claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child (or children).**

Could you please tell me:
1. **How old is your child?** (or if you have multiple children, how old is each child?)

The age categories that matter are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

This helps determine which eligibility rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 7 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 7 years old, they fall into the **under 16** category, which is great news as children under 16 automatically qualify in terms of age.

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Since Alex is only 7 years old, I expect the answer is **no, they are not working** - but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 7!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - Alex is just 7 years old, so they're definitely not working. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)

Again, given Alex's age, I'd expect the answer is that Alex is not receiving any benefits in their own name - but I just need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex is only 7 years old, I would assume they are not in any apprenticeship. Can you confirm that Alex is **not** in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - Alex is only 7, so no apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! Just checking all the boxes. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great progress! Now we need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** AND you're working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ months)
4. You have **pre-settled status** AND you're actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** AND have sufficient financial resources (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have **pre-settled status** AND you're a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant living abroad** (or in certain qualifying countries)

**Do any of these apply to you?** For most people, the first option (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirements. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify as responsible if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** - This is the most common situation
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent) towards their care (e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that covers Alex's upkeep

Does any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but I do help out financially - I contribute towards things like clothes and other bits they need.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (clothes and other necessities), that can count as being responsible for the child - as long as your contributions are at least equal to the Child Benefit amount.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Just to confirm - **do your financial contributions towards Alex amount to at least the Child Benefit rate** (currently around £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child)?

This includes things like money for clothes, food, pocket money, presents, housing costs, etc. - whether paid weekly, monthly, or as occasional lump sums.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's more than enough - £50 per week is well above the Child Benefit rate, so you definitely qualify as being responsible for Alex. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question:**

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Since Alex doesn't live with you, it's possible that someone else (perhaps the person Alex lives with) may already be claiming Child Benefit for them.

Could you tell me:
1. **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for Alex, OR
2. **Someone else is claiming**, but they have agreed to transfer the claim to you, OR
3. **There's a dispute** about who should receive Child Benefit for Alex

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Where does Alex currently live?**

I need to know if any special circumstances apply to Alex's living situation:

1. **In hospital** - Alex is currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - Alex is in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - Alex is in a fostering arrangement
4. **Normal living situation** - Alex lives at home (with another parent, relative, etc.) in a regular domestic setting

Which best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in local authority care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. When a child is in local authority care, there are some additional checks we need to do. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about Alex being in care:**

When a child is in local authority care, Child Benefit can continue in certain circumstances. 

**Does any of the following apply?**

1. **Alex has been in care for 8 weeks or less**, OR
2. **Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you** (even while in care), OR
3. **Alex spends 2 consecutive nights per week at home**, OR
4. **Alex spends 7 or more consecutive days at home** at a time

Does any of these apply to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in care for longer than 8 weeks, and they don't come home to stay with me at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being so open with me about your situation. I understand this must be difficult.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to share this news with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Assessment Result: **Not Eligible**

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why?

While you meet many of the general requirements:
- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ Alex is under 16
- ✅ You contribute financially to Alex's upkeep (£50/week)
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**However**, because Alex has been in local authority care for **more than 8 weeks** and does not spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you, Child Benefit cannot continue.

---

### Important - When You Could Claim Again

You **may be able to claim Child Benefit again** if Alex's situation changes and:
- Alex spends **at least 2 consecutive nights each week** at home with you, OR
- Alex spends **7 or more consecutive days** at home with you

---

### Next Steps

- If Alex's circumstances change (e.g., they start spending regular time at home), you can contact HMRC to discuss making a new claim
- You can reach HMRC's Child Benefit office on **0300 200 3100**
- More information is available at: [gov.uk/child-benefit-for-children-in-hospital-or-care](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-for-children-in-hospital-or-care)

I know this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions or if Alex's situation changes, please don't hesitate to seek further advice.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally support eligibility. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate, making them 'responsible' for Alex. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex is currently in local authority care and has been for longer than 8 weeks. Additionally, Alex does not spend at least 24 hours per week at home with the claimant, nor does Alex spend 2 consecutive nights per week or 7+ consecutive days at home. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care unless the child regularly spends time at home meeting these thresholds. Therefore, the claimant is NOT eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the general eligibility criteria (UK residency, responsible for the child through financial contributions). However, because Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend sufficient time at home, Child Benefit cannot be claimed under current rules.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location

response.reason: Child has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care, unless child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. You can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights each week or 7+ days in a row at home. Contact HMRC about your specific situation.

Therefore, the claimant is NOT eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
